planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 9–12; Spiked Costus is a Caribbean and tropical American native spiral ginger — naturalized in Florida and widely found in Gulf Coast gardens — producing tall, spiraling, leafy canes topped by brilliant scarlet-red, pine-cone-like flower spikes from which small, bright-yellow flowers emerge successively from spring through fall, and thriving in the partial shade of large trees.

directions: Plant Costus spicatus rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade; mulch well to retain moisture and protect the rhizome in zone 9 where frost kills the tops but the roots re-emerge in spring; divide clumps every 2–3 years to maintain vigor and increase; harvest the mature scarlet bracts for use as attractive cut foliage. Spiked Costus is native to the West Indies and adjacent mainland tropical Americas and has become naturalized in moist, disturbed areas of South and Central Florida, where it thrives in partial shade under oaks and palms in the Gulf Coast climate. The spirally arranged leaves on the tall, cane-like stems and the handsome scarlet-red, waxy, bract-covered cone at the tip — from which small, yellow, tubular flowers emerge one at a time — create a bold, tropical effect in the shade garden from late spring through fall. The bracts and stems are decorative in arrangements, lasting well. A highly reliable and tough plant for zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens — dying back to the ground after frost in zone 9 and re-emerging vigorously each spring from the spreading rhizome. Hummingbirds visit the flowers regularly. The stems have been used in traditional medicine in the Caribbean. Naturalizes readily in suitable moist, shaded conditions and can spread to form large colonies.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Costus spicatus
Florida Native Plants Society — Costus spicatus

subcategory: South American tropical spiral ginger ornamental

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 10–12; Rough Spiral Flag is a tall, tropical spiral ginger from the lowland rainforests of Central and South America — bearing rough-textured, spirally arranged leaves on tall canes and distinctive, elongated red-orange bracts with yellow flowers — one of the taller and more architectural of the cultivated Costus species.

directions: Plant Costus scaber divisions in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; provide consistent moisture throughout the warm season and protect from temperatures below 40°F; cut old canes to the ground after they finish blooming and new shoots will emerge from the base; divide clumps every 2–3 years. Rough Spiral Flag is named for the distinctively rough, slightly sandpapery texture of its large, spirally arranged leaves — a texture that sets it apart visually from the smoother-leaved Costus species. The elongated red-orange to brick-red bracts with yellow flowers create a more narrow, torch-like shape than the rounder cones of Costus spicatus. Native to tropical lowland forests and forest margins in Central and South America, it prefers warm, humid conditions in partial shade — making it better adapted to deeply tropical zone 10–12 gardens than the more cold-hardy C. spicatus. Young leaf shoots and tender stem tips of several Costus species, including C. scaber, have been used as edible vegetables by Indigenous peoples throughout their range in tropical America, cooked as pot herbs or eaten raw as salad greens. The plant provides a bold, architectural tropical statement in the garden throughout the warm months and is particularly effective when massed under the canopy of large shade trees.

subcategory: South American tropical white-flower spiral ginger

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 10–12; Guanai Costus is a tall, elegant spiral ginger from the Amazonian lowlands of Bolivia, Brazil, Peru, and Colombia — one of the few white-flowered Costus species — producing long, graceful canes with spirally arranged leaves and terminal white to cream-white flowers with attractive red-flushed bracts in the moist shade of tropical gardens.

directions: Plant Costus guanaiensis divisions in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; provide consistent moisture and warmth; cut old canes to the ground after flowering; divide every 2–3 years to maintain vigor. Guanai Costus (named for Guanai, Bolivia, where it was first collected botanically) is valued in specialist collections for its comparatively rare white flowers in the predominantly orange-red Costus genus. The creamy-white to pure-white flowers with their slightly ruffled petals emerge from oval, green to red-tinged bracts and are produced at the tips of graceful, arching canes with handsomely spiraled leaves. Native to moist, shaded forest floors and streamside habitats in the upper Amazon basin, it is less commonly seen in cultivation than the red-flowered Costus species but performs well in humid, tropical zone 10–12 gardens in partial shade. Like other Costus, the stems are used in traditional medicine throughout the Amazonian range, and some Costus species (though not exclusively C. guanaiensis) have edible young shoots used in indigenous cuisine. The graceful white flowers provide a light, elegant contrast to the more dramatically colored Heliconia and red-bracted Costus in a tropical shade garden composition.
sources: Harvard University Herbaria — Costus guanaiensis Bolivia type

subcategory: African dryland cucurbit ornamental fruiting vine

planting.note: Direct sow or plant tuber divisions in spring in full sun in very well-drained, sandy to loamy, dry to moderately moist soil in zone 9–12; African Cucumber is a drought-adapted cucurbit vine from sub-Saharan Africa — producing charming, small, green-and-white striped, oval fruits that ripen to bright orange and red, and growing from a large, water-storing tuberous rootstock that allows it to survive months-long dry seasons — ornamental and wildlife-valuable in dry gardens and xeriscape plantings.

directions: Plant Kedrostis africana seeds or tuber sections in very well-drained, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; water sparingly during establishment and infrequently once growing — the large tuberous roots store water for extended dry periods; allow to dry down significantly in winter (the plant may appear dead); new growth emerges from the tuber in spring. African Cucumber is one of the most drought-adapted vines in the Cucurbit family — a member of the extraordinary African flora adapted to seasonal drought — growing from a large, knobby, water-storing tuber (caudex) that can persist underground through months-long dry seasons without any rainfall. Slender, twining vines emerge from the tuber in the wet season and bear small, deeply lobed leaves and clusters of tiny, exquisite, green-and-cream-striped oval fruits that look like perfect miniature watermelons as they develop, then ripen through yellow-orange to bright scarlet-red, splitting to reveal black seeds. In African folklore and traditional medicine, the tuberous roots and fruits of related Kedrostis species have various documented uses; the fruits are eaten by birds and mammals in the wild. The ornamental miniature fruits make it a conversation piece in the dry garden; it is well suited to succulent gardens, xeriscapes, and dry South Africa-inspired plantings in zone 9–12 Gulf Coast gardens.
sources: Plants of the World Online — Kedrostis africana
South African National Biodiversity Institute (SANBI) — Kedrostis africana

planting.note: Direct sow seeds after last frost (or 4 weeks before last frost indoors) in full sun to partial shade in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 9–12, annual elsewhere; Green Shiso (Ao Shiso, Green Perilla, Ooba) is the green-leaved variety of Japanese shiso — the essential fresh herb in Japanese, Korean, and Vietnamese cuisines — with large, bright-green, lightly serrated leaves and a distinctive complex flavor combining mint, basil, anise, and cinnamon that is considered by many cooks to be slightly milder and more delicate than the red-leaved variety.

directions: Direct sow Green Shiso seeds on the soil surface (they need light to germinate — do not cover) after last frost in partial to full sun in fertile, well-drained soil; thin to 12 inches; harvest leaves continuously from young plants; pinch flower spikes to extend leaf production, or allow to flower and set seed for next year's crop — plants self-sow abundantly. Green Shiso (Ao Shiso, meaning 'green shiso') is the dominant shiso variety in Japanese cuisine and is used fresh rather than pickled or processed. The large, bright-green, ruffled-edged leaves wrap sushi and sashimi as a palate-cleansing garnish, add their distinctive fragrance to noodle dishes and cold tofus, and are used as crispy tempura in Japanese cooking. In Korean cuisine, green perilla (kkaennip) leaves are eaten fresh with grilled meats, pickled in soy sauce and sesame (kkaennip jangajji), or used to wrap rice. Vietnamese cuisine uses the milder green variety in fresh herb plates with pho and spring rolls. Unlike the purple-red shiso widely used for coloring umeboshi (salted plums), green shiso is typically preferred for fresh herb applications where its bright color and more delicate flavor shine. Self-sows reliably in zone 9–12 gardens after the first year; be prepared for volunteer plants throughout the garden in subsequent seasons. An excellent partial-shade herb for summer kitchen gardens.
sources: Japan Agriculture Cooperative — Perilla frutescens var. viridis cultivation
Ministry of Agriculture Japan — shiso production statistics
Shizuoka Prefecture Agriculture Department — green shiso cultivation

subcategory: Chilean rare true-blue tuberous climbing nasturtium

planting.note: Plant tubers in fall 2–3 inches deep in full sun to partial shade in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, sandy or rocky soil in zone 8–10; Blue Nasturtium is an exceptionally rare Chilean tuberous vine — one of the very few true-blue flowering vines in cultivation — native to rocky, dry hillsides and coastal scrub in Chile where it climbs through shrubs during the cool wet winter season and dies back to its tuberous root in dry summers, bearing small but exquisitely beautiful deep-blue to violet-blue flowers contrasting with white-eyed centers.

directions: Plant Tropaeolum azureum tubers in fall in extremely well-drained, lean, sandy or rocky soil in partial to full sun; the plant grows in cool, wet winters and requires dry summers — absolutely no summer water is essential for tuber persistence; in mild-winter climates (zone 9–10), it grows over winter and early spring as a delicate, wispy vine. Blue Nasturtium is one of the most coveted of all rare bulb collectors' plants — a Chilean vine that produces an abundance of small (3/4 inch) but perfectly formed, intense violet-blue flowers with a characteristic white eye at the center, on delicate, twining stems. True blue is exceptionally rare in flowering plants generally and almost unknown in the Tropaeolum genus (all other nasturtiums produce orange, yellow, red, or pink flowers) — making T. azureum a uniquely prized rarity. Native to the coastal and foothill scrub of central Chile, it grows during the Mediterranean climate winter (cool, moist) and goes completely dormant in the hot, dry Chilean summer; cultivation outside Chile requires closely replicating this regime — autumn planting, winter/spring growth and flowering, then complete summer dryness from June through October. In zone 9 (Gulf Coast), its cool-season growth habit suits the mild winters well and the hot, dry summer dormancy period aligns with the climate; the main challenge is ensuring summer dryness — grow in containers or under an overhang to protect from summer rain.
sources: Universidad de Chile Herbario — Tropaeolum azureum native habitat
